content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322·5662 
George C. Thacher 
City Attorney 
Administratian 
322.5660 
300 Forest Avenue 
Pacific Grove, CA 
93950 
Dear Mr. Thacher: 
Executive/legal 
32~·5901 
Enforcement 
322·6441 
November 7, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322·6444 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-83-226 
Thank you for your request for advice on the disquali-
fication provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
Specifically, 
you asked how the advice in our letter to Thomas Harron, No. 
A-83-184 (copy enclosed), applies to architects who sit on the 
Pacific Grove Planning Commission and Architectural Review 
Committee. 
In our telephone conversation, you stated that the 
architects who are on the planning commission and architectural 
review committee often design new houses or additions to 
existing houses in the area. 
Their clients may request the 
planning commission's approval of, among other things, 
variances, use permits, or subdivision permits. 
In the case of 
the architectural review committee, their clients may ask for 
approval of a shingle, rather than asphalt, roof, or they may 
request approval of a certain facade design. 
In making a request, a client may submit the architect's 
drawings for the project. 
Sometimes the architect appears 
before the agency on the client's behalf; sometimes the 
architect advises the client or the client's representative on 
how to best present the request. 
Your questions are: 
1. 
Is an architect member of a planning commission 
prohibited from assisting a client in the preparation 
of a project to come before the commission? 
2. 
Is an architect member of an architectural review 
committee prohibited from assisting a client in the 
preparation of a project to come before the committee? 


George C. Thacher 
November 7, 1983 
Page 2 
3. 
Does it matter that the architect is handling the 
project on a contingent basis? 
If so, why? 
DISCUSSION 
Government Code Section 871001/ provides that no local 
public official can make, participate in the making, or in any 
way attempt to use his or her official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason 
to know he or she has a financial interest. 
An official has a 
"financial interest" in a decision within the meaning of Section 
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will 
have a material financial effect on: 
* * * 
(c) 
Any source of income ... aggregating $250 or 
more in value provided to, received by, or 
promised to the public official within 12 months 
prior to the time when the decision is made .... 
Section 87103(c). 
A client of an architect becomes a source of income to the 
official once $250 or more is received or promised. 
The 
architect may be paid on a contingent fee basis11 or in some 
other manner. 
If the result of a decision (such as a decision on whether 
to grant a variance or a conditional use permit) will 
foreseeably have a material financial effect11 on either the 
client, or on the income of the official in question, the 
official is required to disqualify himself or herself from 
"making, participating in the making, or in any way attempting 
to use his or her official position to influence" the decision. 
Assuming, as we did in the Harron letter, that the official 
will not "make" or "participate in the making of" the 
11 
Hereinafter all statutory references are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
11 
A future contingent fee is "promised" income within 
the meaning of the Act. 
11 
The phrase "material financial effect" is explained in 
2 cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (copy enclosed) . 


George C. Thacher 
November 7, 1983 
Page 3 
decision,il we focus on whether an official, through his 
activities, will be "using his or her official position to 
influence" the decision. 
This phrase is defined as: 
furthering or attempting to affect in any manner 
any decision: 
(1) 
Within or before his or her agency; or 
(2) 
Before any agency which is appointed by or 
subject to the budgetary controls of his or her agency. 
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700(e) (1), (2). 
If a decision affecting a client's project is before an 
official's agency, or before one subject to his or her agency's 
budgetary or appointive controls, the official in question may 
not appear before the agency on his or her client's behalf. 
The 
official is also prohibited from assisting the client, or the 
client's representative, in the preparation or presentation of 
matters directly related to the request before the agency. 
The 
prohibited activities include helping the client prepare the 
necessary papers for the request, or coaching the client in how 
to most persuasively present the request. 
The architect is not prohibited from working on the 
underlying project so long as his work is not focused on a 
request to his agency or an agency which his agency controls in 
any way. 
Thus, the architect may prepare the drawings for a 
project, even if it is possible that, the drawings will be 
submitted, at some time, to the official's agency, or one 
subject to his or her agency's budgetary or appointive controls, 
as part of a request. 
However, the architect may not prepare 
drawings for the sole or primary purpose of accompanying a 
request before the agency. 
Lastly, you asked whether the manner in which the architect 
is paid affects the outcome. 
Although in the Harron advice 
letter the official was paid on a contingent fee basis, we do 
not feel that the manner of payment is determinative of our 
il See the enclosed copy of 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18700(b), (c) for the definition of these terms. 


George C. Thacher 
November 7, 1983 
Page 4 
advice. 
The same analysis applies when a fee is contingent on 
agency approval or other factors. 
Thank you again for requesting our advice. 
If you have any 
additional questions, please feel free to contact me at 
(916) 322-5901. 
JSM:plh 
Enclosures 
Very truly yours, 
,1"1 
' 
-,CiJ,tU-S 
Janis Shan 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


CITY COUNCIL 
FLORUS C. WILLIAMS 
MAVOR 
JAMES R. HUGHES, D.D.S. 
MORRIS G. FISHER 
MADEL VN A. SLOAN 
DON MARTINE 
OAVID J. EATON 
BOB RUSSELL 
C1T 
o 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
llOO K Street 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Attention: Legal Division 
Dear FPPC: 
ov 
September 29, 1983 
GARV W. BALES 
CITY MANAGER 
FRED SMITH 
ADMIN. SERVICES 
CITY CLERK AND 
C. THACHER 
ATTORNEY 
In the October 1, 1983, "Bulletin" there appears an "advice summary" of 
File No. A-83-184, a letter to Thomas Harron of Chula Vista. 
My questions, 
based on my confusion after reading the summary, are these: 
Is an architect member of a planning commission prohibited from 
assisting a client in the preparation of a project to come before 
the commission? 
Does it matter that the architect is handling the project on a 
contingent basis? 
If so, why? 
I understand that the architect may not appear before the commission (assuming 
no "personal interest" exception) but certainly he may accept projects which 
will be heard by the commission, so long as he doesn't make or participate in 
the making of the decision. 
Please advise. 
Thank you. 
GCT:cb 
Very truly yours, 
J 
/1" 
.if , 
Ceorge C. Thacher 
City Attorney 


.. a 
air Political Practices Commissioll

Dear Mr. Thacher:

322-6441 
October 5, 1983 
Re: 
A-83-226 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political 
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an 
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at 
(916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex 
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to 
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21 
working days.

sections.question: 1. Is an architect member of a planning commission prohibited from assisting a client in the preparation of a project to come before the commission? 2. Is an architect member of an architectural review committee prohibited from assisting a client in the preparation of a project to come before the committee? 3. Does it matter that the architect is handling the project on a contingent basis? If so, why?
sections.conclusion: If a decision affecting a client's project is before an official's agency, or before one subject to his or her agency's budgetary or appointive controls, the official in question may not appear before the agency on his or her client's behalf. The official is also prohibited from assisting the client, or the client's representative, in the preparation or presentation of matters directly related to the request before the agency. The prohibited activities include helping the client prepare the necessary papers for the request, or coaching the client in how to most persuasively present the request. However, the architect may prepare the underlying project drawings so long as the work is not focused on a request to his or her agency. The manner of payment (contingent or otherwise) does not affect this analysis.
sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 871001/ provides that no local 
public official can make, participate in the making, or in any 
way attempt to use his or her official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason 
to know he or she has a financial interest. 
An official has a 
"financial interest" in a decision within the meaning of Section 
87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will 
have a material financial effect on:
sections.question_synthetic: Whether architect members of planning commissions and architectural review committees are prohibited from assisting clients in project preparation when those projects come before their respective agencies, and whether the method of payment (contingent vs. fixed fee) affects this analysis.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Architect members cannot assist clients in preparing or presenting requests before their agency or appear on the client's behalf when a financial interest exists. However, architects may prepare underlying project work and drawings, provided this is not done solely or primarily to accompany an agency request. The method of compensation is not determinative.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Complete advice letter from FPPC Legal Division dated November 7, 1983. Document includes the incoming request (September 29, 1983) and acknowledgment letter (October 5, 1983) as supporting pages. Clear question and conclusion sections present. Analysis references Government Code Section 87100-87103 and administrative code provisions. High confidence extraction with complete verbatim sections available.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Is an architect member of a planning commission prohibited from assisting a client in the preparation of a project to come before the commission? 2. Is an architect member of an architectural review committee prohibited from assisting a client in the preparation of a project to come before the committee? 3. Does it matter that the architect is handling the project on a contingent basis? If so, why?

CONCLUSION: If a decision affecting a client's project is before an official's agency, or before one subject to his or her agency's budgetary or appointive controls, the official in question may not appear before the agency on his or her client's behalf. The official is also prohibited from assisting the client, or the client's representative, in the preparation or presentation of matters directly related to the request before the agency. The prohibited activities include helping the client prepare the necessary papers for the request, or coaching the client in how to most persuasively present the request. However, the architect may prepare the underlying project drawings so long as the work is not focused on a request to his or her agency. The manner of payment (contingent or otherwise) does not affect this analysis.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to Pacific Grove City Attorney's questions about whether architect members of planning commissions and architectural review committees are prohibited from assisting clients with projects coming before their agencies. The FPPC concludes that architects with financial interests from clients must disqualify themselves from influencing agency decisions on those clients' matters, but may perform underlying project work if not done primarily to accompany agency requests.
